coredata.dc:title: A Box-Behnken response surface methodology for optimizing fluorescent detection of cenobamate using graphene quantum dots: Environmental impact assessment and pharmacokinetic applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Almalki, Atiah H.
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Alnemari, Reem M.
Alosaimi, Manal E.
Alaqel, Saleh I.
Serag, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description: Here, a novel spectrofluorimetric method based on functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is proposed for the quantitative determination of cenobamate, a new antiepileptic drug. The method combines the unique optical properties of GQDs, such as excellent fluorescence emission and photostability, with functionalization to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of the probe towards cenobamate. Box-Behnken design was employed as response surface methodology to optimize the experimental conditions, including pH, concentration of GQDs and incubation time. In addition, the method was validated for linearity, precision, accuracy, and robustness. The method displayed excellent linearity in the concentration range 0.25–2 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9998) with a detection limit of 0.0297 μg mL−1. The method also demonstrated high accuracy with mean recovery values of 99.48 ± 0.752 % and satisfactory precision with relative standard deviation values below 2 %. Moreover, the method was successfully validated for the determination of cenobamate in spiked human serum samples, with good recovery values ranging from ranging from 91.65 to 107.48 % and CV% below 5 %. Asides, the developed method shows excellent greenness evaluation, as it eliminates the need for hazardous reagents with simple instrumentation and low waste generation with AGREE score of 0.78. The proposed method has great potential both analytically and clinically, as it was successfully applied for the determination of cenobamate in pharmaceutical formulations and pharmacokinetic studies. This method provides powerful insights into the application of GQDs within the field of pharmaceutical analysis. While the method offers several advantages, including high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and environmental friendliness, it requires careful control of experimental conditions to achieve optimal performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Box-Behnken design
Cenobamate
GQDs
Spectrofluorimetry
AGREE

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for construction of the calibration curve
Method development and optimization using Box-Behnken design
Greenness evaluation of the developed method in comparison with the reported methods
Application of the developed method for pharmaceutical and pharmacokinetic analysis of cenobamate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2024R466), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia and Deanship of Scientific Research at Northern Border University, Arar, KSA for funding this research work through the project number \u201CNBU-FFR-2024-300-02\u201D.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Excitation and emission fluorescence profiles of the modified GQDs demonstrating a strong emission at a λmax of 449 nm following their excitation at 353 nm. (B) Fluorescence emissions spectra observed during the quenching process of the functionalized GQDs with different concentrations of cenobamate.
Stern-Volmer plot illustrating the correlation between cenobamate and the synthesized GQDs at various temperatures.

Numerical optimization of the investigated factors such as pH, GQDs concentration and incubation time on the quenching process of cenobamate and GQDs (R1) showing the best conditions for maximum quenching efficiency.

The competitive selectivity studies involved examining the fluorescence quenching factor [(F0-F)/F0] reactions of the newly created GQDs when interacting with cenobamate and different interfering substances, as well as plasma samples.
Assessing the environmental impact of (A) the developed method, (B) the reported HPLC chromatographic method and (C) the reported LC-MS/MS method using the AGREE tool.
ANOVA table for studying the factors affecting the quenching process between cenobamate and GQDs using Box-Behnken design and applying backward elimination.
Validation data for determination of cenobamate by the developed functionalized GQDs fluorescent probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured on the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured in the three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative analysis of cenobamate in the commercial Xcopri ® tablets by the proposed method and statistical comparison with the reported method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parenthesis are tabulated values of “t “and “F” at (P=0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PK parameters for cenobamate using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: A Box-Behnken response surface methodology for optimizing fluorescent detection of cenobamate using graphene quantum dots: Environmental impact assessment and pharmacokinetic applications

Corresponding author at: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Nasr City 11751, Cairo, Egypt (A. Serag).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a novel spectrofluorimetric method based on functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is proposed for the quantitative determination of cenobamate, a new antiepileptic drug. The method combines the unique optical properties of GQDs, such as excellent fluorescence emission and photostability, with functionalization to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of the probe towards cenobamate. Box-Behnken design was employed as response surface methodology to optimize the experimental conditions, including pH, concentration of GQDs and incubation time. In addition, the method was validated for linearity, precision, accuracy, and robustness. The method displayed excellent linearity in the concentration range 0.25–2 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9998) with a detection limit of 0.0297 μg mL−1. The method also demonstrated high accuracy with mean recovery values of 99.48 ± 0.752 % and satisfactory precision with relative standard deviation values below 2 %. Moreover, the method was successfully validated for the determination of cenobamate in spiked human serum samples, with good recovery values ranging from ranging from 91.65 to 107.48 % and CV% below 5 %. Asides, the developed method shows excellent greenness evaluation, as it eliminates the need for hazardous reagents with simple instrumentation and low waste generation with AGREE score of 0.78. The proposed method has great potential both analytically and clinically, as it was successfully applied for the determination of cenobamate in pharmaceutical formulations and pharmacokinetic studies. This method provides powerful insights into the application of GQDs within the field of pharmaceutical analysis. While the method offers several advantages, including high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and environmental friendliness, it requires careful control of experimental conditions to achieve optimal performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cenobamate is a newly approved medication for managing partial-onset seizures in adults [1]. Although its precise mechanism of action and therapeutic benefits are not completely understood, recent findings indicate that it may block sodium channels, resulting in decreased repetitive nerve cell activity [2]. Moreover, it has been demonstrated to act as a positive allosteric modulator of the gamma-aminobutyric acid channel [3]. These mechanisms make cenobamate a promising option for the treatment of epilepsy and other neurological disorders. Interestingly, cenobamate exhibits complex pharmacokinetics, with a long half-life of 50–60 h in humans, allowing for once-daily dosing [4]. It is primarily metabolized by the liver, through glucuronide conjugation by uridine 5′-diphosphoglucuronosyltransferase (UGT) 2B7 and, to a minor extent,2B4, followed by cytochrome P450 [1]. Regarding its toxicological profile, cenobamate has been associated with drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) syndrome, particularly when initiated at higher doses [5]. This underscores the importance of accurate quantification methods for monitoring plasma concentrations and potential drug-drug interactions. Since the drug is relatively new, there is a need for robust analytical methods to accurately determine its concentration for quantitative analysis and bioanalytical studies. Literature survey revealed that few chromatographic methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with UV detection and liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry were the dominant techniques to quantify cenobamate in pharmaceutical and biological samples, respectively [6–10]. Although these methods are efficient and reliable, they often require expensive equipment, complex sample preparation, and lengthy analysis times. Additionally, the waste generated during the chromatographic conditioning and even in the analysis process may have a negative impact on the environment particularly when not properly disposed.

Spectrofluorimetry, on the other hand, offers a simple, low-cost, and environmentally friendly alternative for chromatographic methods [11]. This technique exhibited several advantages including rapid analysis times, cost effectiveness, and minimal waste generation. Another major advantage of spectrofluorimetry lies in its high sensitivity, allowing for the detection and quantification of analytes at very low concentration [12]. Hence, significant attention in recent years was devoted to analyzing many pharmaceutical compounds using spectrofluorimetry due to its greenness evaluation and potential analytical and economical capabilities [13,14]. One of the recent approaches in this area is the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of many pharmaceuticals, pesticides and even endogenous compounds [11,15–19]. GQDs have emerged as a promising alternative to traditional fluorescent reagents due to their unique optical properties and high photostability, and low environmental impact, making them ideal candidates for sensitive and accurate analysis of pharmaceutical compounds [20,21]. Asides, no derivatization steps are required which simplifies the analytical procedure. However, selectivity of GQDS should be carefully evaluated as some compounds may exhibit interference in fluorescence measurements and quench the fluorescence signal of GQDs. Functionalization of the GQDs can enhance their selectivity and improve their performance as fluorescent probes [22]. This approach has shown promise in the detection and quantification of various compounds, such as Vitamin D and histamine allowing for selective and sensitive analysis of these analytes in biological samples [15,23].

However, different factors such as pH, GQDs concentrations, and incubation time can influence the fluorescence properties of GQDs. Therefore, careful optimization is necessary to ensure accurate and reproducible results. Multivariate design of experiment (DoE) is a chemometric approach that can be utilized to optimize experimental conditions and determine the optimal combination of factors for achieving the desired fluorescence properties of GQDs [24]. Therefore, the goal of our study is to develop a fluorescent probe by functionalizing GQDs for the selective determination of cenobamate, a new antiepileptic drug. We will carefully adjust various factors affecting the method’s sensitivity using a DoE approach, specifically the Box–Behnken experimental design which includes pH, GQDs concentration, and incubation time. Furthermore, we will evaluate the validity of the proposed method in accordance with ICH guidelines for validating analytical procedures and assess its environmental impact using greenness metric tools. The applicability of the developed method will also be assessed in two domains: analysis of pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids for further pharmacokinetic studies.

Cenobamate with a purity of 99.79 % was provided by Metrochem API Pvt Ltd, India. Xcopri® tablets containing 100 mg of cenobamate were obtained from SK life science, Inc. GQDs, sodium tetraphenylborate and acetonitrile were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, while buffer solutions at different pH levels (e.g., pH 3.0, 6.5, and 10) were prepared in accordance with USP protocols.

Functionalized graphene quantum dots were prepared according to our previous work [15,23] as follows: 50 mL of 10−3 M sodium tetraphenylborate aqueous solution was added to 50 mL of 10−3 M cenobamate aqueous solution to form an ion-associated precipitate. After allowing the precipitate to stand in its original solution for 8 h, it was filtered and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, a weighed amount of 100 mg of the dried precipitate was mixed with 100 mg GQDs, dissolved in 50 mL water, and sonicated for 20 min. The volume was then adjusted to 100 mL with water, and the solution was stored at 4 °C until further use.

Measurements were conducted using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer (Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source and a 1 cm quartz cell using the following parameters: excitation wavelength of 353 nm, emission wavelength range of 400–600 nm, and slit width of 5 nm for both monochromators. pH measurements were conducted with a Jenway 3510 pH meter (Jenway, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for construction of the calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of cenobamate was prepared by dissolving 10 mg of the drug powder in 100 mL of water, resulting in a concentration of 100 μg mL−1. This was then diluted with distilled water to obtain a working standard solution with a concentration of 10 μg mL−1. The calibration curve was created by adding various working standard solutions of cenobamate into separate, 10 mL volumetric flasks containing 1.2 mL of functionalized GQDs. After an incubation period for six minutes, the volume was completed with phosphate buffer (pH 6.4) to achieve final concentrations between the range of 0.2–2.0 μg mL−1 for cenobamate. Fluorescent emission intensities were recorded at a wavelength of λem = 449 nm following excitation at λex = 353 nm relative to the blank. The blank solution was prepared in a 10 mL volumetric flask by adding 1.2 mL of GQDs and adjusting to volume with phosphate buffer (pH 6.4). The fluorescence quenching ratios (F°/F) were graphed against the respective drug concentrations (μg mL−1) to generate the calibration curve.

The optimized fluorescent probe method was validated for linearity, limit of detection, limit of quantification, precision, accuracy, and selectivity as per ICH guidelines (Q2(R1)) for analytical method validation [26]. Linearity was evaluated by analyzing seven standard solutions in the range of 0.25 – 2 μg mL−1 cenobamate and determining the correlation coefficient (R2). Detection and quantitation limits were calculated from the calibration curve based on the residual standard deviation of the response and the slope according to the following equations: LOD=3.3 × σ/S and LOQ=10 × σ/S, where σ is the residual standard deviation of the response and S is the slope of the calibration curve. Accuracy and precision were assessed by recovery studies and analyzing replicate quality control samples, respectively, at three different concentration levels (0.5, 0.75 and 1 μg mL−1) in the same day as well as on different days to evaluate the within-day and between-day variations, respectively.

The selectivity of the method was evaluated by analyzing the effect of some excipients commonly found in the pharmaceutical formulation as well as blank samples on the quenching of the functionalized GQDs. Robustness was tested by evaluating the %RSD values after small deliberate changes in the experimental conditions around the optimized values such as pH (±0.1 unit), GQDs concentration (±0.1 mL), and incubation time (±1 min). Furthermore, plasma matrix effect was also evaluated in accordance with ICH M10 guidelines for validating bio-analytical methods [27]. Three concentration levels of cenobamate (0.5, 0.75 and 1 μg mL−1) were spiked to three different lots of blank plasma and the accuracy and precision were calculated to determine the matrix effect.

All experiments were performed in triplicate. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism version 9.0. Student's t-test and F test were used for multiple comparisons. A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Linear regression analysis was used to establish calibration curves.

The greenness of the proposed method was evaluated using the Analytical Greenness (AGREE) metric tool. This tool assesses the method based on the 12 principles of green chemistry, providing a quantitative score for the overall environmental impact of the analytical procedure. The AGREE score was calculated and compared with those of previously reported methods for cenobamate determination.

Ten Xcopri ® tablets were purchased from a local pharmacy in Cairo, Egypt and stored at room temperature (20–25 °C) in their original packaging until analysis. The tablets were within their expiration date and were analyzed within one week of purchase. At the day of analysis, the tablets were weighed and finely powdered. An equivalent amount of 10 mg cenobamate was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in 50 mL water, followed by sonication for 30 min. The volume was then completed with water to obtain a solution labeled to contain 100 μg mL−1 of cenobamate. This solution was filtered, and 10 mL of the filtered solution was transferred to a 50 mL volumetric flask, with the volume being completed with water to obtain a working solution claimed to contain 20 μg mL−1 of cenobamate. Different aliquots of the working solution were transferred to separate 10 mL volumetric flasks and analyzed using the same procedure mentioned earlier to determine the concentration of cenobamate in the tablet samples from the corresponded calibration curve.

All animal procedures were conducted in accordance with international standards for animal care and use, and it was approved by the Ethics Committee in the Faculty of Medicine at Al-Azhar University, Damietta branch (Approval No: DFM-IRB00012367-24–05-009). To evaluate the pharmacokinetics of cenobamate, a single oral dose of 0.27 mg kg−1 was administered to five male Sprague-Dawley rats weighing between 200–250 g. Sprague-Dawley rats were chosen for this study as they are a common outbred rat strain widely used in pharmacological research, including studies on antiepileptic drugs. The dose selection in our study was based on scaling the human equivalent dose to the rat model. Blood samples were collected at various time intervals from 0.5 to 48 h, including a pre-dose sample at time point 0. After collection, the blood samples were immediately centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to obtain plasma and then subjected to protein precipitation using acetonitrile (3 mL). Following vertexing for one minute and subsequent centrifugation at 4000 rpm for another ten minutes, the clear supernatant was dried, reconstituted in water, and finally analyzed using our developed method to determine concentration of cenobamate in rat plasma samples.

The spectral characteristics of the functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for cenobamate were investigated using spectrofluorimetry. These quantum dots exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 449 nm when excited at 353 nm (Fig. 1
A). Such blue emission could be attributed to the presence of functional groups on the graphene quantum dots surface such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, as well as aromatic ring which resulted in electronic transitions between different energy levels within the quantum dots such as π-π* and n-π* transition [28]. Although these transitions could lead to multiple fluorescence peaks, a single broad peak was observed due to the overlapping of these multiple peaks. Interestingly, upon interaction with cenobamate, the fluorescence intensity of the graphene quantum dots decreased significantly, indicating a potential sensing mechanism based on fluorescence quenching (Fig. 1 B). This quenching can be attributed to the strong interaction between the graphene quantum dots and cenobamate molecules, which leads to the transfer of energy from the excited state of the quantum dots to the cenobamate molecules, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity.

Therefore, the sensing mechanism between the functionalized GQDs and cenobamate needs to be investigated. To tackle this issue, a Stern-Volmer plot was constructed by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) against the concentration of cenobamate (Fig. 2 ). The plot revealed a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and the concentration of cenobamate, with an increased response as temperature rises, indicating a dynamic quenching process. It is worth mentioning that the inner filter effect on the quenching process was negligible, as confirmed by the absorption spectra of cenobamate, which showed minimal overlap as no significant absorbance was observed within the fluorescence emission wavelength range of the graphene quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method development and optimization using Box-Behnken design
ce:section.ce:para:
Regarding the validity of the quadratic model, several validation parameters were employed to assess the model performance, including lack-of-fit test, predicted R2, and CV%. The lack-of-fit test was found to be insignificant (p > 0.05), suggesting that the quadratic model adequately fit the data. In addition, there was close agreement between the predicted R2 (0.88) and the adjusted R2 (0.96), indicating good predictability of the model. Moreover, CV% values were found to be low i.e. 2.34 %, indicating good precision and reproducibility of the model. Other diagnostic graphs such as residual plots were examined to ensure the adequacy of the model ( Fig. S2
&S3). These diagnostic graphs confirmed that the quadratic model was appropriate for capturing the relationships between the studied parameters and the quenching process of cenobamate.

Another interesting capability of applying DoE in optimization studies is the ability to visualize the effects of different factors on the response variable. 3D surface plots were generated to illustrate the interaction between pH, GQDs concentrations, and incubation time on the quenching process of cenobamate. Fig. S4 reveals the interaction between pH and GQDs concentration. The pronounced curvature in the surface plot indicates a nonlinear relationship between pH and the quenching process. Optimal quenching efficiency is achieved at intermediate pH levels, around 6–7. This can be attributed to the favorable protonation states of both GQDs and cenobamate within this pH range, which facilitates their interaction. The observed decrease in quenching efficiency at more extreme pH values is likely due to changes in the ionization states of the molecules, which subsequently affects their binding affinity. In addition, Fig. S5 depicts the relationship between GQDs concentration and incubation time. The interaction appears more linear, with higher GQDs concentrations corresponding to greater quenching of fluorescence. This suggests that within the examined concentration range, the quenching process does not reach a saturation point, and increasing GQDs concentrations continue to provide additional binding sites for cenobamate. Additionally, incubation time exhibits a positive correlation with quenching efficiency, indicating a time-dependent interaction between GQDs and cenobamate. However, the effect seems to plateau at longer incubation periods, implying that the majority of binding interactions take place within the first few minutes of the process. The observed interaction effects, as demonstrated by these response surfaces, underscore the critical importance of thoroughly optimizing these parameters to maximize the sensitivity of cenobamate analysis. For example, while elevating the GQDs concentration generally enhances the quenching effect, this impact is modulated by the pH level, underscoring the intricate interplay between these factors in determining the overall method performance.

Validation of the proposed method was conducted in accordance with ICH guidelines [26] to ensure its accuracy, precision, selectivity, linearity, and robustness (Table 2 ). Linearity of the proposed method was determined using seven calibrator points covering a concentration range of 0.25–2 μg mL−1. The calibration curve exhibited good linearity with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9998 indicating a strong relationship between the concentration of cenobamate and the fluorescence intensity ratio of the quenched GQDs. The sensitivity of the proposed method was also evaluated based on the limit of detection and limit of quantitation. The method displayed LOD and LOQ values of 0.0297 and 0.0900 μg mL−1, respectively, indicating the capability of the method to detect and quantify cenobamate at a nanogram level concentration.

Accuracy and precision of the method were assessed through recovery studies and intra-day and inter-day precision experiments at three different concentration levels of cenobamate (0.5, 0.75 and 1 μg mL−1). The method demonstrated good accuracy with mean recovery value of 99.48 ± 0.752 % and satisfactory intra and interday precision with relative standard deviation values lower than 2 %, indicating the method's reliability and reproducibility (Table 2). The robustness of the method was evaluated by assessing its performance under small variations in experimental conditions such as pH, concentration of GQDs and incubation time (Table 2). The results showed that the proposed method had good robustness, as slight changes in these parameters did not significantly affect the accuracy and precision of the measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness evaluation of the developed method in comparison with the reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: Greenness evaluation of the developed method was carried out to assess its environmental impact in comparison with other reported methods for the determination of cenobamate. An initial assessment revealed a significant biocompatibility and eco-friendliness of the proposed method owing to three major factors. The first factor is the use of GQDs which are known to be biocompatible and environmentally friendly materials. The second factor is using water as solvent in the preparation of standard solutions as well as pharmaceutical samples. The third factor is the employed spectrofluorometric detection technique, which is known for its energy efficiency and minimal generation of waste compared to other analytical techniques such as mass spectrometry or chromatography in addition to high throughput where many samples can be analyzed in a short amount of time.

However, in order to evaluate the method greenness in a more quantitative way, AGREE tool has been employed which assess the method greenness based on the 12 principals of green chemistry (Fig. 5 ) [29]. Interestingly, the developed method shows an AGREE score of 0.78, indicating a high level of greenness and adherence to the principles of green chemistry. The AGREE evaluation of the reported HPLC chromatographic method [6] disclosed a lower greenness score of 0.68 which suggests a lower level of environmental friendliness compared to the developed method. This could be attributed to factors such as the use of organic solvents such as acetonitrile as a mobile phase, longer run times, and generation of more waste. Moreover, our developed method was also compared with another reported LC-MS/MS method for cenobamate determination [9], which showed also a lower greenness score of 0.57. Additional penalties were given to this method also due to the expensive and more energy intensive nature of LC-MS/MS analysis which requires the use of multiple solvents, longer analysis times, and generates more waste compared to the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the developed method for pharmaceutical and pharmacokinetic analysis of cenobamate
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed method for the analysis of cenobamate in pharmaceutical samples as well as for pharmacokinetic studies was also investigated. The method successfully quantified cenobamate in Xcopri® tablets with a mean recovery of 100.83 % and a %RSD of less than 1.5 %, demonstrating its accuracy and precision for the analysis of pharmaceutical samples (Table 3 ). Moreover, the results were also statistically evaluated using an independent t-test and F test, which showed no significant difference between the developed method and a reported chromatographic method [6] pinpointing the reliability and equivalence of the developed method for cenobamate analysis in pharmaceutical samples. Besides, these results are comparable to those reported by Damodharan et al [7], who used HPLC for cenobamate analysis in tablet formulations and achieved recoveries of 99.38–100.93 %. Our method demonstrates similar accuracy while offering advantages in terms of simplicity and reduced analysis time.

In conclusion, this study successfully developed and validated a novel spectrofluorimetric method using functionalized GQDs as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of cenobamate. Optimization using Box-Behnken design yielded optimal conditions of pH 6.4, GQDs concentration of 1.2 mL, and incubation time of 5.8 min, achieving a desirability value of 0.97 for maximum quenching efficiency. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9998) in the range of 0.25–2 μg mL−1, with high sensitivity (LOD=0.0297 μg mL−1, LOQ=0.0900 μg mL−1). Validation studies showed high accuracy (mean recovery 99.48 ± 0.752 %) and precision (RSD<2%) for cenobamate quantification. Notably, the method achieved an AGREE score of 0.78, indicating superior greenness compared to reported HPLC and LC-MS/MS methods. Its successful application in pharmaceutical analysis yielded a mean recovery of 100.83 % (RSD<1.5 %) for Xcopri® tablets, while pharmacokinetic studies in rats revealed rapid absorption (Cmax = 1.03 μg mL−1 at 2 h) and a long elimination half-life (t½ ≈ 35 h), consistent with human studies. Despite these achievements, limitations such as careful control of experimental conditions to achieve optimal performance, the need for further selectivity studies in complex biological matrices and larger-scale human pharmacokinetic investigations should be addressed. Future research should focus on validating the method across a broader range of pharmaceutical formulations and patient populations, conducting comparative studies with other analytical techniques such as LC-MS/MS, and exploring its potential for automation in high-throughput clinical settings. Overall, the developed method offers a valuable tool for both pharmaceutical quality control and therapeutic drug monitoring of cenobamate.

This research was funded by Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2024R466), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia and Deanship of Scientific Research at Northern Border University, Arar, KSA through the project number “NBU-FFR-2024-300-02”.

Atiah H. Almalki: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Maram H. Abduljabbar: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Reem M. Alnemari: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Manal E. Alosaimi: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Saleh I. Alaqel: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Ahmed Serag: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2024R466), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia and Deanship of Scientific Research at Northern Border University, Arar, KSA for funding this research work through the project number “NBU-FFR-2024-300-02”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111381.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
